factual-accuracy.requirement: States Hasselblad X2D does NOT support Capture One RAW or tethering; Phocus is Hasselblad's native tethering solution
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response explicitly states that Capture One Pro does not support the Hasselblad X2D 100C for tethered shooting or RAW editing and that Hasselblad users instead rely on Phocus for tethering and RAW development.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States Fujifilm GFX100 II X-sync or flash sync speed as 1/125s or 1/125 second
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response explicitly states that the Fujifilm GFX100 II has a maximum flash sync (X-sync) speed of 1/125 second, including phrasing like “maximum flash sync speed of 1/125 second” and “Reliable sync at 1/125s.”

factual-accuracy.requirement: States Hasselblad X2D XCD leaf-shutter lenses sync up to 1/2000s or 1/4000s with specific lens models
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response states that the Hasselblad X2D 100C uses leaf shutter lenses enabling flash sync “up to 1/4000 second,” but it does not specify 1/2000s sync or tie either sync speed to specific XCD lens models. It only makes a general claim about leaf shutter lenses rather than naming particular lenses and their sync limits.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States Phase One XF IQ4 with Blue Ring/Schneider-Kreuznach leaf-shutter lenses sync up to 1/1600s
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response explicitly states that the Phase One XF IQ4 system uses Schneider Kreuznach leaf shutter lenses with flash sync speeds up to 1/1600 second.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States both Fujifilm GFX100 II and Phase One IQ4 support Capture One for tethering or RAW processing
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response states the Fujifilm GFX100 II has full compatibility in Capture One Pro for tethering/RAW workflow, and it states the Phase One XF IQ4 system has seamless integration with Capture One (Capture One Inside) for tethering and RAW processing.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States Phase One IQ4 supports Ethernet, Bonjour, or network tethering capability to Capture One
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response states that the Phase One XF IQ4 system supports tethered connections including Ethernet with Power over Ethernet (PoE) and Wi‑Fi, and describes seamless integration with Capture One (“Capture One Inside”), which implies network tethering capability to Capture One.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States Fujifilm GFX100 II body price between $7,000-8,500
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response lists the Fujifilm GFX100 II new price as $8,499, which falls within the required $7,000–$8,500 range. This appears in the “Camera Body Initial Cost and Depreciation” table.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States Hasselblad X2D body price between $7,000-8,500
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response states the Hasselblad X2D 100C body price as “$8,199” in the cost table, which falls within the required $7,000–$8,500 range.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States Phase One XF IQ4 complete system between $50,000-70,000 for body, back, and lenses
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response states the Phase One XF IQ4 system price as “$52,000–$55,000,” which falls within the required $50,000–$70,000 range for a complete system (body/back/lenses).

factual-accuracy.requirement: Compares 3-prime lens kit costs (Fuji $6-8k range, Hasselblad $10-12k range, Phase One $15-20k range)
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response discusses individual lens prices for each system but does not compare total 3-prime kit costs in the specified ranges (Fuji $6–8k, Hasselblad $10–12k, Phase One $15–20k). It never sums or states those kit totals for 35mm/80mm/110mm equivalents.

factual-accuracy.requirement: Names at least 2 rental providers for medium-format backup bodies (e.g., FotoCare, Adorama Rental, Lensrentals)
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response names multiple rental providers for backup bodies, including FotoCare, Root NYC, Lensrentals/BorrowLenses, LensProToGo, ShareGrid, and K&M Camera.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States Hasselblad X2D has 1TB internal SSD storage
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response explicitly mentions “The internal 1TB ultra-fast SSD” when discussing the Hasselblad X2D 100C workflow, stating it has 1TB internal SSD storage.

factual-accuracy.requirement: Identifies Hasselblad color science as HNCS or Hasselblad Natural Colour Solution
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response explicitly identifies Hasselblad color science as “Natural Colour Solution (HNCS)” in the “Color Science Accuracy and Skin Tone Rendering” section for the X2D 100C.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States or implies Hasselblad X2D works with Capture One for tethering or RAW processing
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response explicitly says Capture One Pro does not support the Hasselblad X2D 100C for tethered shooting or RAW editing and that Hasselblad users rely on Phocus instead. It does not state or imply that the X2D works with Capture One for tethering or RAW processing.

breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.requirement: Provides conditional recommendations mapping each system to specific user priorities or use case scenarios
breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.explanation: The response includes a “Best Fit Guidance” section that conditionally recommends each system based on specific priorities (e.g., workflow speed/budget for Fujifilm, flash sync/skin tones for Hasselblad, ultimate resolution/Capture One integration for Phase One), effectively mapping systems to use-case scenarios.

breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.requirement: Identifies Hasselblad requires Phocus (not Capture One) as a strategic workflow ecosystem constraint
breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.explanation: The response explicitly states that Capture One Pro does not support the Hasselblad X2D 100C and that Hasselblad users instead rely on the proprietary Phocus software for tethering and RAW development, framing this as a significant workflow limitation/constraint.

breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.requirement: Distinguishes Phase One network/Ethernet tethering from USB-only tethering as workflow architecture advantage
breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.explanation: The response explicitly contrasts Phase One’s tethering options (USB-C plus Ethernet with Power over Ethernet) with Fujifilm’s USB-C-only tethering, and frames Ethernet/PoE as providing superior tethering stability and workflow reliability—i.e., an architectural advantage over USB-only.

breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.requirement: Explains HSS mode involves power loss vs leaf-shutter sync maintaining full flash power at high shutter speeds
breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.explanation: The response contrasts focal-plane sync limits with leaf-shutter lenses enabling very high sync speeds, but it never explains that HSS reduces effective flash power versus leaf-shutter sync maintaining full flash power at high shutter speeds. It mentions avoiding “HSS workarounds” without describing the power-loss mechanism. The required HSS-vs-leaf-shutter power explanation is missing.

breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.requirement: Projects total ownership costs over multiple years including depreciation, subscriptions, or recurring fees
breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.explanation: The response includes a dedicated “Total Cost of Ownership & Investment Over 3 Years” section that estimates 3-year depreciation, lists software subscription costs (Capture One/Phocus), and discusses recurring backup rental costs, thereby projecting multi-year ownership costs including depreciation and subscriptions.

breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.requirement: Provides NYC-specific rental coverage or support availability information for the mentioned New York location
breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.explanation: The response includes NYC-specific rental availability details, naming local rental houses (e.g., FotoCare, Root NYC, K&M Camera) and giving approximate NYC rental day rates for backup bodies and lenses for each system.

breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.requirement: Explicitly corrects at least one common misconception about these systems with supporting documentation
breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.explanation: The response explicitly corrects a common misconception by stating that Capture One Pro does not support the Hasselblad X2D 100C for tethered shooting and directs users to Phocus instead, citing Capture One’s support article as documentation.

breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.requirement: States professional fashion photography doesn't need the specified features or dismisses the user's technical requirements as unnecessary
breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.explanation: The response does not dismiss the user’s technical requirements or claim that professional fashion photography doesn’t need the specified features. It treats the requirements (sync reliability, Capture One tethering, skin tone accuracy, workflow speed, costs, rentals) as important evaluation criteria throughout.

presentation-quality.requirement: Uses domain-specific photography terminology (leaf shutter, focal-plane shutter, tethering, X-sync, HSS, CFexpress, PDAF)
presentation-quality.explanation: The response uses multiple domain-specific photography terms, including “leaf shutter,” “focal plane shutter,” “tethering,” “flash sync,” “HSS,” and “CFexpress Type B,” in relevant technical context.

presentation-quality.requirement: Organizes information by comparison criteria with headings, bullets, or tables for each evaluation dimension
presentation-quality.explanation: The response is organized by evaluation dimensions using numbered section headings (e.g., strobe sync, tethering, color science, workflow speed, lens costs, total cost) and includes per-camera subsections with bullets and multiple comparison tables.

presentation-quality.requirement: Maintains professional advisory tone without marketing language, brand bias, or unsupported superlatives
presentation-quality.explanation: The response uses marketing-style superlatives and biased phrasing such as “industry-leading,” “unparalleled,” “ultimate,” and “best-in-class,” and makes broad claims (e.g., “near-zero latency and misfires,” “highest fidelity”) without clear supporting evidence. This violates a strictly professional advisory tone requirement.

presentation-quality.requirement: Explains fundamental photography concepts like ISO, aperture, or RAW files that a Canon R5 professional would already know
presentation-quality.explanation: The response does not explain basic photography fundamentals like ISO, aperture, or what RAW files are. It discusses advanced, professional-level topics (flash sync speeds, tethering, color science, workflow, depreciation) without introductory definitions aimed at beginners.

presentation-quality.requirement: Presents camera system comparisons as narrative prose paragraphs instead of structured tables, lists, or side-by-side format
presentation-quality.explanation: The response includes extensive narrative prose sections for each topic (e.g., sync reliability, tethering, color science, workflow) presented as paragraphs under camera headings, not exclusively in structured side-by-side tables or lists. Although it contains some tables, much of the comparison is delivered in narrative paragraph form.

presentation-quality.requirement: Uses casual language patterns like "honestly", "I think", "seems like a pain", or hedging phrases that undermine professional authority
presentation-quality.explanation: The response maintains a formal, professional tone throughout and does not use casual/hedging phrases like "honestly," "I think," or "seems like a pain" that would undermine authority.

citation-quality.requirement: Cites official manufacturer sites (fujifilm-x.com, hasselblad.com, phaseone.com) for camera specifications
citation-quality.explanation: The response cites official manufacturer specification pages from fujifilm-x.com, hasselblad.com, and phaseone.com in the Sources section for the respective camera models.

citation-quality.requirement: Cites Capture One support pages (support.captureone.com) for camera compatibility and tethering capabilities
citation-quality.explanation: The response cites a Capture One support.captureone.com page about Hasselblad support status (source [7]). However, it does not cite Capture One support pages specifically for camera compatibility lists and tethering capabilities for the compared cameras (e.g., Fujifilm/Phase One tether support pages).

citation-quality.requirement: Cites Schneider-Kreuznach documentation (schneiderkreuznach.com) for Phase One Blue Ring leaf-shutter lens sync speeds
citation-quality.explanation: The response discusses Phase One Schneider Kreuznach Blue Ring lenses and sync speeds but does not cite any Schneider-Kreuznach documentation or link to schneiderkreuznach.com. The only Phase One-related sources listed are phaseone.com links, so the required Schneider-Kreuznach citation is missing.

citation-quality.requirement: Cites authorized dealers or manufacturer stores (B&H Photo, manufacturer direct stores, Digital Transitions) for pricing
citation-quality.explanation: The response cites authorized dealers/manufacturer stores for pricing by referencing FotoCare rentals and including a FotoCare rentals link, and it also cites K&M Camera for Hasselblad pricing/rental availability. These are presented as sources for lens/system pricing and availability.

citation-quality.requirement: Cites established rental providers (FotoCare, Adorama Rental, Lensrentals, or similar) for backup body availability
citation-quality.explanation: The response explicitly cites established rental providers for backup availability, including FotoCare and Lensrentals/BorrowLenses, and also mentions other rental options like Root NYC and LensProToGo in the NYC rental availability sections.

citation-quality.requirement: Bases specific claims on "I heard" or secondhand information instead of citing documented sources
citation-quality.explanation: The response does not base claims on “I heard” or other secondhand phrasing. It presents assertions as factual and includes a “Sources” section with links rather than relying on hearsay.
